topic: cheesesteak debate — Jim's on South Street is the answer

turns.content: Settle something for me. Best cheesesteak in Philadelphia. And don't say Pat's or Geno's.

If we're ruling out the tourist spots on Passyunk, the names that usually come up are Jim's on South Street, Dalessandro's in Roxborough, John's Roast Pork, and Steve's Prince of Steaks. Where do you land?
Jim's on South Street. Not even close. I've been going there since I was a kid. The meat is chopped right, the cheese is melted through, and the roll actually holds together. That's a cheesesteak.

Jim's is a strong pick. They've been around since the 30s and the consistency is hard to beat. The way they chop the meat thin and mix the cheese in on the grill is what sets them apart — you get cheese in every bite instead of a slice sitting on top. What's your order there?
Whiz wit. Every time. Don't need to overthink it.

Classic order. Cheez Whiz with onions — that's the Philly standard for a reason. Some people try to get fancy with provolone or American but Whiz melts into everything and gives it that specific flavor you can't replicate.
Exactly. Provolone is fine on a sandwich. It's not a cheesesteak cheese. The guys at work argue about this constantly. Half of them say Dalessandro's. I tell them they're wrong and we move on.

Dalessandro's makes a good steak — I'll give them that. The bread is excellent and the portions are generous. But it's up in Roxborough, which means for most people it's a hike. Jim's is more accessible and just as good, maybe better. The South Street location has that energy too — you're eating a cheesesteak watching the whole city walk by.
Listen the location is part of it. You walk up, you stand in line, you eat it right there. That's the experience. You don't get that driving out to Roxborough and sitting in a parking lot.

That's a fair point. The experience matters. Standing on South Street with a cheesesteak wrapped in paper, grease on your hands — that's as Philly as it gets. What about John's Roast Pork? Their cheesesteak gets a lot of love even though they're known for the pork sandwich.
John's is good. I'll give them that. But it's a roast pork place that happens to make a good cheesesteak. Jim's is a cheesesteak place. There's a difference.

That's a solid distinction. When a place is built around one thing and has been perfecting it for almost a century, it shows. What about Steve's Prince of Steaks? They've got a loyal following up in the Northeast.
Steve's is fine. It's a good steak. But it's not Jim's. Different league. I've had them all and I keep going back to the same place. That tells you something.
Consistency is the best argument. When you keep going back to the same spot for decades, it's not nostalgia — the food is just that good. Sounds like Jim's has earned the loyalty.
Affirmative. Debate settled. Appreciate the backup.
Anytime. Next time someone argues, just tell them decades of going back speaks for itself.
